title: Zonisamide in the Treatment of Painful Diabetic Neuropathy: A Randomized, Double-Blind, Placebo-Controlled Pilot Study
abstract: Background. Painful diabetic neuropathy is a form of neuropathic pain frequently encountered as a complication of diabetes mellitus types I and II. Pharmacotherapy is one modality of treatment for this distressing and often disabling condition, but there is no medication available that consistently provides adequate pain relief with acceptable safety and tolerability. Tricyclic antidepressants, certain antiepileptic drugs, and opioids have been shown in randomized, controlled trials to be of benefit in painful diabetic neuropathy, although none has Food and Drug Administration (FDA)approved labeling for this indication. Study Objective. To analyze the safety and efficacy of zonisamide in the treatment of painful diabetic neuropathy. This pilot study is the first randomized, controlled trial of zonisamide for the treatment of any neuropathic pain disorder. Study Design. Forty-two patients 18-80 years of age with type I or type II diabetes mellitus and at least a 3-month history of painful diabetic neuropathy were screened in the study, and 25 were randomized to zonisamide (N = 13) or placebo (N = 12). The study drug was titrated over a 6-week period and continued at a fixed dosage for a 6-week maintenance period. The mean dosage of zonisamide for the maintenance phase was 540 mg/day. Outcome Measures. Patients kept a daily log of their pain using both a 0-100 mm visual analog scale and a 0-10 Likert scale. Result. Pain scores on both the visual analog scale and the Likert scale decreased more for the zonisamide group compared with the placebo group, regardless of whether the comparison was made for the intent-to-treat population, the population that entered the maintenance phase, or the completer population, but these differences did not reach statistical significance. Tolerability of zonisamide was only fair in this study, which had a high number of dropouts from the zonisamide group. Conclusion. A larger randomized, controlled trial is needed to establish the efficacy and tolerability of zonisamide for painful diabetic neuropathy.
text: Background. Painful diabetic neuropathy is a form of neuropathic pain frequently encountered as a complication of diabetes mellitus types I and II. Pharmacotherapy is one modality of treatment for this distressing and often disabling condition, but there is no medication available that consistently provides adequate pain relief with acceptable safety and tolerability. Tricyclic antidepressants, certain antiepileptic drugs, and opioids have been shown in randomized, controlled trials to be of benefit in painful diabetic neuropathy, although none has Food and Drug Administration (FDA)approved labeling for this indication. Study Objective. To analyze the safety and efficacy of zonisamide in the treatment of painful diabetic neuropathy. This pilot study is the first randomized, controlled trial of zonisamide for the treatment of any neuropathic pain disorder. Study Design. Forty-two patients 18-80 years of age with type I or type II diabetes mellitus and at least a 3-month history of painful diabetic neuropathy were screened in the study, and 25 were randomized to zonisamide (N = 13) or placebo (N = 12). The study drug was titrated over a 6-week period and continued at a fixed dosage for a 6-week maintenance period. The mean dosage of zonisamide for the maintenance phase was 540 mg/day. Outcome Measures. Patients kept a daily log of their pain using both a 0-100 mm visual analog scale and a 0-10 Likert scale. Result. Pain scores on both the visual analog scale and the Likert scale decreased more for the zonisamide group compared with the placebo group, regardless of whether the comparison was made for the intent-to-treat population, the population that entered the maintenance phase, or the completer population, but these differences did not reach statistical significance. Tolerability of zonisamide was only fair in this study, which had a high number of dropouts from the zonisamide group. Conclusion. A larger randomized, controlled trial is needed to establish the efficacy and tolerability of zonisamide for painful diabetic neuropathy.
main_text: Introduction iabetes mellitus (DM) is reaching epidemic proportions in the industrialized world [1] . The incidence of type II DM is rising dramatically, primarily as a result of the increasing prevalence of overweight and obesity in children and adults [2] . Using data from the National Health Interview Survey, the estimated lifetime risk of developing DM for those born in the United States in 2000 is 32.8% for males and 38.5% for females [1] . In 1999 the overall prevalence of this disease in the U.S. was 6.9%, and for those over 69 years of age it was 14.4% [2] . Among those diagnosed with DM, up to 24% will develop painful diabetic neuropathy (PDN) [3] , which suggests that as many as 4 million adults in the U.S. alone may suffer from this complication. There is insufficient epidemiologic evidence to define clearly what the risk factors are for PDN, although cohort studies and clinical experience implicate older age, duration of diabetes, and elevated glycosylated hemoglobin [3] . There is also conflicting data from small case series on the natural history of the disorder, so it is not well understood to what extent the pain remains constant, improves, or progresses over time [4] [5] [6] . However, in a survey of patients living in the community, the average pain intensity was 5.75 on the Likert scale (0-10, worst pain = 10), and the degree of interference with work, recreation, mobility, and sleep was substantial [7] . Given these data, the medical need for safe and effective pharmacotherapy for PDN is clear. Studies performed in the 1980s and early 1990s demonstrated that tricyclic antidepressants possessed modest efficacy in PDN [8] [9] [10] . Amitriptyline was the most frequently studied drug [9] [10] [11] [12] , but desipramine was also shown to be effective in randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trials [10, 13] , and imipramine showed positive results in two open-label [8, 14] and one comparison trial [8] . In one randomized, controlled trial, fluoxetine did not differ from placebo, and further investigation of selective serotonin uptake inhibitors as analgesics has not been pursued [10] . Despite the drawbacks, tricyclic antidepressants are still recommended as the first-line agents in the treatment of PDN, although they do not have a specific Food and Drug Administration (FDA)-approved indication for PDN [15] . The other major classes of drugs that have been studied in the treatment of PDN are opioids and antiepileptics. Both tramadol and controlled-D release oxycodone have had positive results in controlled trials [16, 17] . A search of the peerreviewed, English-language literature from 1980 to 2003 identified only six randomized, doubleblind, placebo-controlled trials of an antiepileptic drug for PDN: three of gabapentin [18] [19] [20] , two of valproate [21, 22] , and one of lamotrigine [23] . All of these studies showed some degree of efficacy for the tested antiepileptic drug. In addition, the pathophysiology of PDN has been shown in animal research to consist, at least in part, of upregulation of sodium channels in nociceptive neurons [24] [25] [26] . Zonisamide has been shown to prolong the inactive phase of voltage-dependent sodium channels, thereby inhibiting the propagation of action potentials, a mechanism of action it shares with several other antiepileptic drugs [27] . Therefore, it was anticipated that zonisamide would also have a beneficial effect in the treatment of PDN. This study represents the first randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial of zonisamide for the treatment of any neuropathic pain condition. Zonisamide was approved for use in the U.S. in 2000 as adjunctive treatment for partial seizures in adults [28, 29] and is generally well tolerated in both children and adults [30, 31] . It has been shown in open-label studies to benefit patients with neuropathic pain [32] [33] [34] . In addition to the blockade of voltage-dependent sodium channels [27] , zonisamide possesses other mechanisms of action that may also contribute to relief of neuropathic pain [35] . Its long half-life (63 hours), which allows for once-daily dosing and more stable serum levels when doses are missed, is also advantageous in long-term treatment [27] .MethodsStudy Population The eligible population consisted of adults 18-80 years of age with either type I or type II DM. The primary inclusion criteria were a ≥ 3-month history of PDN and pain severity ≥ 40 mm on a visual analog scale (VAS) (0-100, worst pain = 100) and/or ≥ 4 on a Likert scale. Patients were excluded from the study if they had a neurological disorder other than PDN, including a seizure disorder for which they were taking antiepileptic drugs. Additional exclusion criteria were a skin condition in the area affected by the diabetic nerve pain that could alter sensation, such as an open, unhealed ulcer; an amputation other than toes or fingers; a history of chronic infectious illness such as hepatitis B, hepatitis C, and HIV; a history of drug and/or alcohol abuse within the prior year; an immunosuppressive condition such as cancer; a history of active kidney or ureter stones within the past year; a history of kidney disease or liver disease; a creatinine clearance < 50 mL/minute; symptomatic psychiatric illness; a sulfonamide allergy; any prior treatment with zonisamide; or any treatment with an experimental medication within the prior month.Study Design This was a single-center, randomized, doubleblind, placebo-controlled pilot study to assess the safety and efficacy of zonisamide in the treatment of PDN. It was determined that 12 subjects in each group would give an 80% chance of detecting a difference of two points on an 11-point Likert scale when holding alpha = 0.05, using an estimated standard deviation of 1.8 and using a onesided test. We had initially intended to randomize 15 patients to each group to account for dropouts, but practical considerations limited the final sample size to 25. There was a 1-to 2-week screening phase prior to randomization, a 12-week doubleblind phase (6 weeks of titration and 6 weeks of fixed dose), and a 2-week follow-up phase subsequent to drug discontinuation.Screening Phase Patients who met the inclusion and exclusion criteria and who gave informed consent were entered into a 1-or 2-week screening phase (depending on whether an extra week was needed to complete discontinuation of prohibited medications). At the initial visit, each patient had a physical exam (including neurological exam, vital signs, and weight) and provided blood samples for chemistries, complete blood count (CBC), liver function tests (LFTs), glycosylated hemoglobin (HGB A 1C ), and urine samples for pregnancy and toxicology tests. Each patient completed the Short Form McGill Pain Questionnaire, a daily log, and VAS rating (Table 1 ). If patients were taking any benzodiazepine, antiepileptic drug, or tricyclic antidepressant, they were required to taper off these medications by the date of the initial visit of the screening phase of the study, and the screening phase was then extended to 2 weeks. This was done in order to avoid any confounding effects of these medications on pain measured during the treatment phase of the study. However, patients were allowed to continue on a stable dose of any analgesic medication that they had been taking regularly, including opioids, throughout all phases of the study. Similarly, the patient's medications for diabetes also were continued at a stable dose during the study. The only drug allowed on an as-needed basis for pain was acetaminophen. Baseline ratings for pain, sleep, and activity level were established using the daily log. This two-page document had the VAS pain and sleep scales on page 1 and the Likert pain and activity scales on page 2. The second page also contained a record of the number of study pills taken, the number of pain medications taken (permitted analgesics plus acetaminophen prn), and the number of hours slept. Only patients whose mean pain severity logged during the screening phase was at least 40 mm on the VAS and/or at least 4 on the Likert scale, with day-to-day variability of < 25%, were entered into the study. Patients who met all the criteria throughout the screening phase were assigned to the placebo or active-drug group in a double-blind fashion. Patients were randomized in blocks of 6. Of the 42 patients who entered the screening phase, 25 were randomized to receive study drug or placebo. The remaining 17 patients were ineligible, or chose not to enter the study, for the following reasons: psychiatric history (1), positive toxicology for cocaine (2) , elevated glycosylated hemoglobin (3), insufficient pain (6) , inability to discontinue a prohibited medication (gabapentin) (1), an unrelated health problem (1), and no explanation given (3).Six-Week Titration Phase On day 1 patients had their second study visit, during which they filled out the Pain and Impairment Relationship Scale, the Functional Interference Estimate, the Short Form-36 Health Survey, and the Short Form McGill Pain Questionnaire. After completing these instruments, patients were administered 100 mg of zonisamide or placebo. The placebo capsule contained a small amount of lactulose, and the zonisamide capsule was doubleencapsulated so as to appear identical to the placebo. The dose of zonisamide was titrated in the first 6 weeks by 100 mg every 7 days, to a maximum dosage of 600 mg/day, or until the patient reached maximum relief of pain or started having significant side-effects. When intolerable sideeffects occurred, the dose was reduced to the last tolerated dose. The minimum target dosage of zonisamide was 300 mg/day. Compliance was monitored closely by doing complete pill counts at each clinic visit. Patients were also prompted in the daily diary to answer the questions "Did you take your study medication today?" and "How many did you take?" Patients were instructed to fill out the two-page daily logs for the duration of the study and to bring the forms with them to each clinic visit. Patients' intensity of pain, daily functioning, and quality of life were assessed at visit 5 (day 42, the final day of the titration phase), using the same instruments as on visit 1. Blood samples for chemistries, CBC with differential, and LFTs, and urine samples for toxicology were also obtained at this visit.Six-Week Maintenance Phase During this phase the maximum tolerable dose of zonisamide or placebo was continued. Blood sam-ples for chemistries, CBC with differential, and LFTs, and urine samples for toxicology were obtained on visit 7 (day 84) at the conclusion of this phase. Patients' intensity of pain, daily functioning, and quality of life were also assessed at this visit, using the same instruments as on visit 1. A urine sample for pregnancy was also collected at visit 6 (day 56).Two-Week Follow-up Study medication was discontinued abruptly at the start of this phase. Patients continued to fill out the daily forms during this phase. At visit 8 (day 96, final day of the study) they had a physical exam and completed the entire battery of questionnaires.Study Analysis PopulationsEfficacy Measurements Pain scores for each week were determined by taking the average of the daily pain ratings on both the VAS and the Likert scale. The primary measure of efficacy was the difference between the baseline pain score (the screening phase) and the mean of the weekly pain scores for the maintenance phase with last-observation-carried-forward imputation for the ITT-E population. (Missing weekly mean scores from the 6-week maintenance phase were imputed with the last recorded weekly mean score after the start of treatment.) Secondary efficacy analyses were performed using available data; the first compared the baseline pain score with the mean of the weekly pain scores for the population who participated in the maintenance phase and the second compared the baseline pain score with the final week's pain score for the population who completed the study. Based on a sample size of 15 evaluable patients per group, the study had 80% power to detect a difference between groups (alpha = 0.05, two-sided t -test) of two Likert points, assuming a standard deviation of 1.84. Although 42 patients were entered into the study, only 25 met criteria for randomization, which compromised the power of the study. A post hoc analysis was also performed using a ≥ 50% reduction in pain score as the cutoff for response. This latter measure has been used as an alternative definition of response in other pain studies [23] and to identify those patients with the best outcomes.Safety and Tolerability Measurements The incidence, degree of relatedness to study drug, and severity of treatment emergent adverse events were evaluated for the ITT-S population. Safety was evaluated at each of eight clinic visits by the monitoring of vital signs and laboratory values and the recording of any changes in general health as elicited by detailed history, as well as by documentation of any serious medical event occurring during study participation. Any adverse event deemed to be serious was considered a safety issue. Any visit to the emergency department or admission to the hospital was defined as a serious adverse event and reported to the Institutional Review Board (IRB). Tolerability was evaluated by noting the frequency and severity of adverse events and by a clinical assessment by the lead author (A.A.) of the likelihood that they were related to the study medication.Data Analysis Mean Likert and VAS scores were calculated for each week throughout the study. Grand means (the mean score for each phase of the study) were obtained by averaging the weekly mean scores recorded in each phase. Prior to the calculation, missing weekly mean scores from the 6-week maintenance period were imputed with the last recorded weekly mean score preceding the missing value, even if it was from the titration phase. Therefore, change from pretreatment to maintenance was determined for each patient who had any treatment data. A t -test was used for the treatment comparisons of change in the Likert and VAS mean scores. Several alternative approaches to data analysis reinforced the results of the primary analysis. These included a comparison of the change in pain scores from baseline to the maintenance phase grand mean for observed data (only patients who entered the maintenance phase) and a comparison of the change in pain scores from baseline to the final week of the maintenance phase (only patients who completed the study). The proportion of patients in each treatment group who achieved a 50% reduction in pain was also evaluated as a measure of treatment success. This comparison was made using Fisher's exact test. Exact 95% confidence intervals (CI) were determined for each response rate using the binomial distribution. The incidence of treatment emergent adverse events was evaluated in the ITT-S for each body system. Neurological adverse events were identified separately because the study drug was an antiepileptic. Statistical significance for the between-group comparison of each adverse event category was determined by using Fisher's exact test.Results Twenty-five patients were randomized to treatment: 13 to zonisamide and 12 to placebo. The demographics and pain features of each group were similar (Table 2 ). Five of the 13 patients randomized to zonisamide and 11 of the 12 patients randomized to placebo completed the study; 12 patients in each group received at least one dose of study drug (Figure 1 ). Study completers in the zonisamide group did not differ on initial pain level from study noncompleters (6.6 ± 1.5 vs 6.2 ± 0.7). One patient from the pla- cebo group was dropped from the study at week 3 for opening the double-encapsulated study medication, a violation of the study protocol. Six patients in the zonisamide group discontinued the study during the titration phase because of death unrelated to the study (1), a serious drugrelated adverse event (drug rash) (1), headache on day 2 (1), headache during week 2 (1), irritability and insomnia during week 3 (1), and time constraints during week 2 (1). Thus, seven patients in the zonisamide group completed the titration phase to 600 mg/day. Two had dosage adjustments during the maintenance phase: one to 500 mg/day and the other to 400 mg/day. Two patients on 600 mg/day dropped out during the maintenance phase: one because of an unrelated medical problem and the other because of jitteriness and a metallic taste.Efficacy The primary measure of efficacy was the reduction from baseline in pain scores on the VAS and the Likert scale in the ITT-E population, using the mean score for the 6-week maintenance period with last-observation-carried-forward imputation. The mean ( ± SD) VAS score in the zonisamide group (N = 11) decreased from 58.9 ± 9.4 to 41.2 ± 21.2, a reduction of 17.7 ± 19.0; in the placebo group (N = 12) it decreased from 63.9 ± 18.1 to 57.0 ± 19.9, a reduction of 6.9 ± 15.4 ( P = 0.15). In the zonisamide group the mean ( ± SD) score on the Likert scale decreased from 6.45 ± 1.1 to 4.89 ± 2.1, a reduction of 1.56 ± 1.9; in the placebo group it decreased from 6.63 ± 1.7 to 6.03 ± 1.7, a reduction of 0.60 ± 1.4 ( P = 0.18). Two alternative approaches to assessing pain reduction were also employed. In the first of these, only those who remained in the study until at least the first week of the maintenance phase were included in the analysis, which were seven patients in the zonisamide group and 11 patients in the placebo group. In this analysis, the mean pain score on the VAS decreased by 25.1 ± 18.9 for the zonisamide group compared with 7.9 ± 15.7 for the placebo group ( P = 0.05). The mean pain score on the Likert scale decreased by 2.1 ± 2.0 for the zonisamide group compared with 0.7 ± 1.5 for the placebo group ( P = 0.10). The second alternative analysis included only those who completed the study, which were five patients in the zonisamide group and 11 patients in the placebo group. In this analysis the mean pain score on the VAS decreased by 21.5 ± 22.3 for the zonisamide group compared with 8.8 ± 17.4 for the placebo group ( P = 0.23). Similarly, the mean pain score on the Likert scale decreased by 1.6 ± 2.3 for the zonisamide group compared with 0.7 ± 1.6 for the placebo group ( P = 0.35). Thus, although there was a similar trend favoring zonisamide in each of these efficacy analyses, the difference between placebo and zonisamide did not meet the original criteria for meaningful response, which was a difference of at least 2 on the Likert scale and/or 20 mm on the VAS. The positive trend is best illustrated by a graph that plots the weekly mean change in VAS scores for each group over the entire course of the study (Figure 2 ). The zonisamide group separates from the placebo group at week 4 of the titration phase, sustains a steady difference through the end of the maintenance phase, and loses this effect by the end of the washout phase. The figure also displays the attrition in the zonisamide cohort that occurred over the course of the study. A post hoc responder analysis was performed using as the criterion for response a ≥50% reduction in pain from baseline to the final week of study participation. In the ITT-E there were 3 of 11 responders in the zonisamide group (27%; 95% CI, 6-61%) and 0 of 12 responders in the placebo group (0%; 95% CI, 0-26%) (P = 0.09). Among those patients who entered the maintenance phase of the study 3 of 7 in the zonisamide group were responders (43%; 95% CI, 10-82%) and 0 of 11 in the placebo group were responders (0%; 95% CI, 0-28%) (P = 0.04) (Figure 3 ). Although the small sample size and the post hoc nature of these data make any inference tentative, it is noteworthy that three patients in the zonisamide group responded, whereas none in the placebo group did. In addition to pain scores, subjects were assessed using a variety of instruments designed to measure other important variables such as sleep, daily functioning, pain interference, and general health. No significant differences were found between baseline and study completion in either of the groups, nor were there any significant differences between the two groups on any of these measures. A larger sample size would be required to improve the chance of finding differences on these secondary measures.Safety and Tolerability One patient died from a myocardial infarction that was deemed to be entirely unrelated to the study drug. One patient was dropped from the study because of a serious adverse event (drug rash without systemic complications) from which the patient recovered completely soon after discontinuation of the zonisamide. No significant changes in laboratory values were observed over the course of the study in either the zonisamide or the placebo group. Glycemic control fluctuated throughout the study, but there were no significant differences in blood glucose levels between baseline and final measurement in either group. There were differences between groups for several of the treatment emergent adverse events reported (Table 3 ). Total neurological adverse events did not differ between groups, but dizziness, jitteriness, restlessness/insomnia, and balance/equilibrium occurred only in zonisamidetreated patients, while hallucinations and blackout spells occurred only in placebo-treated patients. The difference in the incidence of treatment emergent adverse events reached statistical significance (P = 0.04) only for the gastrointestinal system, but all of the adverse events in this category were mild to moderate. Of the eight patients in the zonisamide group who dropped out of the study, four probably had drug-related adverse events as the primary cause. These adverse events were headache (1), irritability and insomnia (1), jitteriness and a metallic taste (1), and rash (1) . The adverse events that necessitated dosage adjustment but did not lead to study discontinuation were drowsiness (2) and nausea/vomiting (1) . Response is defined as 50% or greater pain reduction from baseline. Likert scale and VAS scores yield identical results for response. The responder rate is indicated in parentheses. Intent-to-treat = analysis with imputation for all patients receiving study medication. Observed data = analysis of observed data for all patients who participated in the maintenance period of the study for at least 1 week.  Weight changes are of major importance in the management of type I and type II DM, because weight loss has a beneficial impact on the course of the disease, while weight gain is associated with exacerbation of the illness. In this study no patient reported weight gain; however, three patients in the zonisamide group reported weight loss compared with one in the placebo group. Although there were no statistically significant differences in weight change between the groups based on the vital sign data, of the three patients on zonisamide who lost ≥2 kg by the end of the maintenance phase (mean weight loss, 3.7 kg), the two who went on to complete the follow-up phase of the study had each gained back at least 1 kg during the 2 weeks off drug.Discussion To the authors' knowledge this is the first randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled study on the use of zonisamide in the treatment of PDN or any other pain disorder. The results of this pilot study suggest that the drug may be an effective analgesic for this condition. All of the analyses showed a consistent trend in favor of zonisamide. Whereas three patients in the zonisamide intentto-treat group had ≥50% pain relief, none of the patients in the placebo group had this result, despite the fact that 11 of 12 patients in that group actually completed the study. This important result, combined with the observation that the time course of improvement in the drug-treated group was synchronous with the titration of the drug, is clinically significant. Zonisamide was shown to have a good safety profile, but tolerability was only fair in this study. Of the eight patients in the zonisamide group who did not complete the study, four had drug-related adverse events that contributed to their withdrawing from the study. However, in two of these cases, the patients discontinued the drug without notifying the staff, which precluded the option of negotiating a dosage reduction. One of these patients had reached the maintenance phase and had obtained substantial pain reduction. Furthermore, except for mild-to-moderate gastrointestinal side-effects, there were no significant differences in the occurrence of adverse events between the drug and the placebo groups. The authors believe that tolerability would have been better if the drug were titrated more slowly, perhaps 100 mg/day every 2 weeks for the first several dose increases, and if the target dose were limited to 500 mg/day except in those patients who failed to respond. One strength of this study was its 12-week duration. Previous controlled studies of antiepileptic drugs in the treatment of neuropathic pain conditions have not exceeded 8 weeks of drug administration [18, 22, 23] . The extended duration of the study demonstrated the time course of what may have been a response to the drug. Putative benefit appeared midway through the titration phase, remained stable throughout the maintenance period, and was lost after the 2-week washout phase. Other strengths of the study were the careful sample selection process, the rigorous assessment procedures and data analysis, and the intensive monitoring of medication compliance. The main weakness of the study, however, was the high dropout rate in the zonisamide group. This low completion rate diminished the statistical power of the trial. The groups did not differ significantly on any of the secondary measures, even though questionnaires and sleep scales yielded a large amount of data. Although this may be due in part to the small sample size, it also suggests that any differences found between the groups on the pain measures were unique to pain and not part of a more generalized, nonspecific change. There was a slight trend toward weight loss in the zonisamide group, and two patients had a clear weight gain during the 2-week follow-up washout period after having lost more than 2 kg while on zonisamide. This parameter should be studied more carefully in any future trials of zonisamide in patients with DM because of the substantial benefit of weight loss and the equally detrimental effect of weight gain. Zonisamide has been shown to promote weight loss in at least one wellcontrolled trial [36] and this effect was also found in a number of epilepsy trials [37] . It is unclear whether this effect of zonisamide on weight reduction is a direct or indirect effect of the drug.Conclusion Future research with a larger sample will be necessary to determine whether zonisamide is an effective, safe, and well-tolerated agent for the treatment of PDN. For example, a randomized clinical trial with 160 patients, comparing the rates of ≥50% reduction in pain between a zonisamidetreated group and a placebo-treated group, would be able to detect a 20% difference between groups with 80% statistical power using a two-tailed chi-squared test (with continuity correction) at a = 0.05, given a 10% placebo response and an early dropout rate £10%. Until more definitive evidence is available, however, the results of this pilot study suggest that zonisamide may be worthy of consideration in patients with PDN who have not responded to, or have some contraindication to, standard pharmacotherapy.1 . 1 The intent-to-treat population for the analysis of efficacy (ITT-E) included patients who received study drug and had at least one posttreatment efficacy evaluation (N = 23, 11 in the zonisamide group). 2. The intent-to-treat population for the analysis of safety (ITT-S) included patients who received a dose of study drug (N = 24, 12 in the zonisamide group).3. The completer population consisted of thosepatients who completed the maintenance phase of the study (N = 16, 5 in the zonisamide group). 4. The maintenance phase participation population consisted of those patients who provided at least 1 week of data from the maintenance phase (N = 18, 7 in the zonisamide group).Figure 1 1 Figure 1 Flow chart of the randomized, controlled trial.Figure 3 3 Figure 3 Patient response during the maintenance period.Response is defined as 50% or greater pain reduction from baseline.
